skill.name: pptx
skill.description: Create, edit, inspect, and validate PowerPoint presentations using PPTX, HTML, and OOXML workflows.
skill.summary: Work with PowerPoint files through HTML conversion, OOXML editing, validation, and thumbnail generation.
skill.author: ArtemisAI

security_audit.summary: The scanner reported many severe patterns, but most C2, weak crypto, network scanning, and Ruby backtick alerts are false positives from OOXML schemas, documentation, or JavaScript template strings. Real risk remains from unsafe ZIP extraction of user-supplied Office files and local automation that runs document converters and renders HTML. No evidence found of prompt injection or confirmed malicious data exfiltration, so the skill is not blocked but should not be published without fixes.

high_findings.title: Unsafe Office Archive Extraction
high_findings.description: The skill extracts user-supplied Office ZIP containers with extractall. A crafted archive can write files outside the intended output directory through ZIP path traversal.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: Direct extractall calls operate on Office files, which are ZIP archives supplied by users. No path normalization or member validation is visible at the cited locations.

medium_findings.title: External Tool Execution on User Documents
medium_findings.description: The scripts launch LibreOffice, Poppler, and git through subprocess.run. The commands use argument arrays rather than shell strings, but they still execute local binaries on user-controlled documents.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are confirmed, but command injection risk is reduced because shell=True is not used. The remaining risk is parser exposure and local process execution on untrusted files.

medium_findings.title: Local HTML Rendering Without Network Controls
medium_findings.description: The HTML conversion workflow opens a local file in Playwright. If untrusted HTML is rendered, embedded scripts or remote resources may execute during conversion.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file:// navigation is explicit and no network blocking or script disabling is shown. Legitimate generated HTML is expected, so exploitability depends on whether untrusted HTML is accepted.

medium_findings.title: Privileged Dependency Installation Instructions
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs users to install LibreOffice and Poppler with sudo apt-get. This is not malicious, but marketplace users should review privileged installation steps before running them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The sudo commands are directly present in dependency instructions. They install common document tools, so the risk is operational rather than evidence of malicious intent.

low_findings.title: Environment Variable Use Appears Limited to Temporary Directory Selection
low_findings.description: The environment access finding is a low-risk configuration use of TMPDIR. No evidence found that environment secrets are read or sent elsewhere.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited code and documentation only reference TMPDIR for temporary file placement. I did not find evidence of credential access or exfiltration at these locations.

low_findings.title: Static Keyword Matches in OOXML Schemas and Documentation
low_findings.description: Many C2, weak crypto, network scanning, path traversal, and URL findings are OOXML vocabulary, XML namespace URLs, or documentation examples. These are false positives in context.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are schema enumeration names, relationship paths, and standard OpenXML namespace constants. They do not indicate command-and-control logic, cryptography, or network scanning behavior.

low_findings.title: Documentation Backticks Misclassified as Shell Execution
low_findings.description: The large number of Ruby backtick findings in Markdown and SKILL.md are code fences, inline paths, XML examples, or JavaScript template strings. They are not Ruby shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited Markdown uses fenced commands and inline formatting, while the JavaScript line uses a template literal. These are not Ruby backtick execution contexts.

dangerous_patterns.title: Unsafe Office Archive Extraction
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill extracts user-supplied Office ZIP containers with extractall. A crafted archive can write files outside the intended output directory through ZIP path traversal.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Direct extractall calls operate on Office files, which are ZIP archives supplied by users. No path normalization or member validation is visible at the cited locations.

dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts launch LibreOffice, Poppler, and git through subprocess.run. The commands use argument arrays rather than shell strings, but they still execute local binaries on user-controlled documents.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The commands are confirmed, but command injection risk is reduced because shell=True is not used. The remaining risk is parser exposure and local process execution on untrusted files.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local HTML Rendering Without Network Controls
dangerous_patterns.description: The HTML conversion workflow opens a local file in Playwright. If untrusted HTML is rendered, embedded scripts or remote resources may execute during conversion.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file:// navigation is explicit and no network blocking or script disabling is shown. Legitimate generated HTML is expected, so exploitability depends on whether untrusted HTML is accepted.

dangerous_patterns.description: The skill instructs users to install LibreOffice and Poppler with sudo apt-get. This is not malicious, but marketplace users should review privileged installation steps before running them.

content.value_statement: PowerPoint work often requires both visual slide creation and low-level file inspection. This skill provides workflows for creating, editing, validating, and analyzing PPTX files.
actual_capabilities: Create PowerPoint slides from HTML layouts with pptxgenjs.
Inspect and edit raw OOXML files inside PPTX archives.
Extract presentation text through markitdown workflows.
Generate slide thumbnails using LibreOffice and Poppler tools.
Validate presentation packages and compare document changes.
Guide replacement, rearrangement, notes, comments, and layout edits.
limitations: Requires external tools such as LibreOffice, Poppler, Playwright, and pptxgenjs.
Raw OOXML edits can break a presentation if relationships are updated incorrectly.
Untrusted PPTX files should be handled in a sandbox because archive extraction is used.
Complex PowerPoint animations and proprietary effects may not convert perfectly.

use_cases.title: Create a Client Presentation
use_cases.description: Generate a new slide deck from structured content and a visual direction.

use_cases.title: Update an Existing Deck
use_cases.description: Replace text, rearrange slides, add notes, or adjust layouts in an existing PPTX file.

use_cases.title: Audit Presentation Structure
use_cases.description: Inspect OOXML relationships, media files, comments, speaker notes, and validation errors.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Simple Deck
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a 6-slide PowerPoint about [topic]. Use a clear structure, readable text, and a professional visual style.

prompt_templates.title: Edit an Existing Presentation
prompt_templates.prompt: Open [file.pptx], update the title and key points for [audience], and preserve the existing theme and layout.

prompt_templates.title: Analyze Design and Content
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze [file.pptx] for slide structure, typography, colors, comments, notes, and layout issues. Summarize recommended fixes.

prompt_templates.title: Perform OOXML-Level Repair
prompt_templates.prompt: Unpack [file.pptx], inspect the OOXML relationships and content types, fix the broken slide references, validate the package, and repack it.

output_examples.input: Create a quarterly business review deck for a product team.
output: A polished PPTX deck with title, agenda, metrics, roadmap, risks, and next steps slides.
Speaker notes that explain the main talking points for each slide.
A short summary of design choices and any assumptions used.

output_examples.input: Inspect an existing presentation for hidden notes and comments.
output: A slide-by-slide summary of visible text, speaker notes, and comment locations.
A list of embedded media and layout files used by the deck.
Warnings about missing relationships or files that may affect playback.

output_examples.input: Convert a designed HTML slide into a PowerPoint slide.
output: A PPTX slide that preserves text placement, colors, shapes, and image positions.
Validation feedback if content overflows or does not match the presentation layout.

best_practices: Work on a copy of the original presentation before editing raw OOXML files.
Use a sandbox when processing PPTX files from unknown sources.
Validate and open the final deck after every structural change.
anti_patterns: Do not run privileged dependency installation commands without review.
Do not unpack untrusted Office files into sensitive directories.
Do not edit slide XML without updating related relationship and content type files.

faq.question: Can this skill create a PowerPoint file from scratch?
faq.answer: Yes. It uses HTML and pptxgenjs workflows to build new PPTX presentations.

faq.question: Can it edit an existing presentation?
faq.answer: Yes. It can inspect and modify raw OOXML files, then repack the presentation.

faq.question: Does it require extra software?
faq.answer: Yes. Some workflows require LibreOffice, Poppler, Playwright, pptxgenjs, markitdown, and Python packages.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with unknown PPTX files?
faq.answer: Use a sandbox for unknown files. The scripts unpack Office archives and run local conversion tools.

faq.question: Can it preserve a brand template?
faq.answer: It can inspect themes, fonts, colors, layouts, and masters to help preserve a template.

faq.question: Can it repair broken presentations?
faq.answer: It can help repair relationships, content types, and XML structure, but complex corruption may need manual review.
